coredata.dc:title: A novel dual emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 to achieve Cu2+ detection in water environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Liping
Guo, Hao
Wu, Ning
Liu, Yinsheng
Liu, Bingqing
Wang, Mingyue
Chen, Yuan
Tian, Jiaying
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was synthesized by post-synthesis modifying of UiO-66, a zirconium-based MOF formed by 1,4-phthalic acid (PTA) and Zr4+ , with europium ion (Eu3+) and carbon dots (CDs) and utilized for the determination of Cu2+ ions. The prepared composites not only maintain the excellent optical properties of CDs and Eu3+ but also have good stability in an aqueous solution. At the excitation wavelength of 299 nm, Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 has two distinct emission peaks at 394 nm and 615 nm, respectively. More interestingly, when the material was dispersed in an aqueous solution containing Cu2+ ions, the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 615 nm decreased significantly, while the characteristic peak of CDs at 394 nm remained unchanged. In the range of 0–500 μM, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F394/F615) linearly increased with increasing Cu2+ ion concentration, and the detection limit was calculated to be 51 nM (3Sb/Ksv). At the same time, the material can also be effectively applied to the detection of Cu2+ in environmental water samples. So Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 can be developed as a potential ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect Cu2+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66
Cu2+ detection
Environmental water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ in an aqueous solution
Determination of Cu2+ in actual water samples
Optimization of reaction temperature and time
Characterization of CDs, CDs@UiO-66 and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66
Particle size and zeta potential distribution
Detection of Cu2+ in the actual water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21665024 ), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province ( 20JR10RA083 ), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan ( NWNU-LKQN-18-23 ), the Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of CDs prepared at different temperatures (a) and different reaction times (b).
(a) TEM and (b) SEM images of CDs, (c) CDs@UiO-66, and (d) Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66; (e) EDS images of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66.

(a) XRD patterns of the simulated UiO-66 (orange) and the as-prepared UiO-66 (blue), CDs (black), CDs@UiO-66 (red) and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 (purple); (b) FTIR spectra of UiO-66 (blue), CDs (black), CDs@UiO-66 (red) and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 (purple); (c)TGA plot of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66; (d) XRD patterns of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 treated in aqueous solution for different days.
(a) Particle size distribution and zeta potentials of CDs, CDs@UiO-66 and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (black line), excitation (blue line) and emission spectra (red line) of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs@UiO-66 and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 and (c) fluorescence emission spectra of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 suspension under different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence emission spectra and relative fluorescence intensities of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 in different storage days (a, b), pH (c, d) and NaCl concentrations (e, f).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 in aqueous solutions of different metal ions and (b) the corresponding ratiometric fluorescence intensities (F394/F615), (c) influence of Cu2+ on fluorescence emission spectrum of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 (The insets are optical photographs before and after the addition of Cu2+ to Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 suspension under the UV-lamp) and (d) the corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram before (X1 =0.298，Y1 =0.149) and after Cu2+ (X2 =0.191, Y2 =0.082) is added.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 in the presence of different Cu2+ concentrations and (b) the linear relationship of the ratio fluorescence intensity (F394 /F615) and concentration of Cu2+.
Schematic diagram of Cu2+ ion sensing mechanism.
Synthesis of the CDs, CDs@UiO-66, and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66.
Comparison of the sensing performance of the proposed sensor with other reported fluorescent sensors for the detection of Cu2+.
Determination of Cu2+ content in actual water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A novel dual emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 to achieve Cu2+ detection in water environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE; Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province; College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou Northwest Normal University, P R China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was synthesized by post-synthesis modifying of UiO-66, a zirconium-based MOF formed by 1,4-phthalic acid (PTA) and Zr4+ , with europium ion (Eu3+) and carbon dots (CDs) and utilized for the determination of Cu2+ ions. The prepared composites not only maintain the excellent optical properties of CDs and Eu3+ but also have good stability in an aqueous solution. At the excitation wavelength of 299 nm, Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 has two distinct emission peaks at 394 nm and 615 nm, respectively. More interestingly, when the material was dispersed in an aqueous solution containing Cu2+ ions, the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 615 nm decreased significantly, while the characteristic peak of CDs at 394 nm remained unchanged. In the range of 0–500 μM, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F394/F615) linearly increased with increasing Cu2+ ion concentration, and the detection limit was calculated to be 51 nM (3Sb/Ksv). At the same time, the material can also be effectively applied to the detection of Cu2+ in environmental water samples. So Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 can be developed as a potential ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect Cu2+ ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of science and technology and industrialization, heavy metal ion pollution is becoming more and more serious. In particular, due to the continuous development and progress of manufacturing, pharmaceutical, chemical, and other industries in recent years, heavy metal ions have yielded a continuous negative impact on the natural environment [1]. Heavy metal ions are a kind of widespread pollutants that can accumulate through the food chain and excessive dose ingestion will inflict serious damage to human health. Once ingested into the human body, they will coordinate with important substances in the body, changing the normal physiological function of the substance, and even entering the nervous system to interfer with the normal function of the nerve. Therefore, recognition and quantification of the heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ , Cd2+ and Cr6+ in environmental water samples are very important [2–5]. Among them, Cu2+ ion is an essential trace element for human body, which is necessary for the growth and development of human body [6], but excessive Cu2+ residues in the body can have negative effects on various organs, especially the liver and gallbladder. This will lead to many diseases, for example, gastrointestinal diseases, Alzheimer's disease, liver and kidney damage, and so on [7–9]. World Health Organization set the maximum allowable level of Cu2+ in drinking water as 30 μM, while the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) sets the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water to be only 20 μM [10]. Consequently, from the perspective of environmental protection and human health, the development of a new material and technology for rapidly and sensitively monitoring of Cu2+ content will be very crucial for solving the global concern of Cu2+ pollution. Nowadays, there have been many methods to detect Cu2+ ions, for example, flame atomic absorption spectrometry[11], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES)[12], laser ablation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (LA-ICPMS) [13], UV–visible spectrophotometry [14] and electrochemistry [15], and so on. But these methods usually require complicated pre-processing and expensive equipment [16], so it is very important to establish a convenient, sensitive and selective monitoring method for Cu2+ in environmental water samples [17,18].

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention due to their high fluorescence quantum efficiency, low cost, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [19–25], and have been widely used in medical treatment [26], cell imaging [27] and chemical and biological sensors [28]. Up to now, there have been many reports on the preparation of CDs using biological raw materials, such as garlic [29], spinach [30], actinidia deliciosa [31], lemon juice [32], pericarp [33], pine fruit [34] and bagasse pulp [35]. These natural substances are composed of a large number of proteins, carbohydrates and other organic matter, which can provide plentiful carbon atoms and heteroatoms for the preparation of carbon dots [36,37]. Therefore, using biological materials to synthesize CDs has attracted more and more researchers’ interest. It is well-known that Pinellia ternata, which is part of the Araceae family, is a valuable medical plant [38]. It has been reported that Pinellia tuber contains a large amount of alkaloids, proteins, iridoids, glycosides, anthraquinones, fatty acids and their derivatives, and other organic compounds [39–41], so it is a good precursor material for the preparation of CDs.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are crystalline porous materials composed of metal ions or metal clusters and organic ligands by coordination bonds. Due to good load capacity, large specific surface area, controllable aperture, adjustable emission characteristics and predictable nanostructure [42], they have been widely used in many fields, such as gas adsorption and separation [43], catalysis [44] and chemical sensing [45–47]. In recent years, luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) have been widely used in the field of sensing because of their key advantages in structural characteristics, functional components and interaction with analytes, and developed into chemical sensors for various analytes [48]. Although LMOF has made great progress in the field of chemical sensors, it also has some limitations. Most established LMOF sensors rely on a single emission center for quantitative analysis of analytes, which may produce positive or negative errors. In order to avoid these defects, it is necessary to design and synthesize ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on multi-emission center MOFs composite. By simultaneously measuring two or more response signals, more accurate built-in correction results can be achieved, thus effectively overcoming environmental disturbances, including fluctuations of light source or detector and changes in probe concentration, so the ratiometric fluorescence sensor is considered as a promising candidate for chemical sensing [49–51]. It is an ideal sensor design scheme to introduce some luminescent materials whose optical properties are affected by the targets into MOFs but to keep the main structure and properties of MOFs unchanged. On this account an encapsulation strategy has attracted great attention from researchers [52–54]. The composite sensor with both MOFs properties and the optical properties of nanoparticles was fabricated by combining various types of luminescent nanoparticles with MOFs crystals [55–57]. Moreover, Lanthanide metal organic framework materials (Ln-MOFs) have also attracted extensive interest due to their high luminescence quantum yields and long fluorescence emission lifetime. However, the increase of lanthanide concentration in Ln-MOFs maybe lead to cross relaxation between activators, eventually leading to fluorescence intensity reduction and even quenching [45]. The post-synthetic modifications can compensate for the limitations of Ln-MOFs mentioned above, and lanthanide ions can be used as luminescent elements like CDs to construct fluorescence sensors. Considering that the energy transfer from organic ligands to Eu3+ can usually realize the photosensitization process and Eu3+ characteristic fluorescent emission locates in red region, which is conductive to visual analysis, we select Eu3+ with red emission and CDs with blue emission as modifying agents for UiO-66 crystals.

Based above consideration, in this paper, a dual-emission fluorescence sensor based on MOFs was prepared by via post-synthetic modification for visual detection of Cu2+ with the advantages of simplicity, rapidity, high sensitivity and wide linear range. At first, green and environmentally friendly CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method with Pinellia ternata as raw material for the first time and were encapsulated in UiO-66. Then, a dual-emission Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was obtained by modifying the resulted composite material with Eu3+. It displays an obvious fluorescence color change (red→blue) and can achieve visual detection of Cu2+ in UV lamp. The proposed sensor has higher sensitivity and wide linear range than recently reported similar ones. The dual-emission Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 sensor provides a new approach for the rapid visual detection of Cu2+ in water environment.

CDs were prepared from Pinellia ternate by a simple hydrothermal method. Typically, 1.2 g of dried Pinellia ternate was ground into powder and added 120 mL of deionized water. The obtained suspension was sonicated for 30 min. Then, the mixtures were transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. At the end of the reaction, the mixture was filtered to obtain a uniformly dispersed brown CDs solution. Finally, the CDs solution was further purified by dialysis membrane (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove unnecessary products in the prepared CDs solution. The pale-yellow solution of the CDs was stored at 4 °C for further use in later experiments.

CDs@UiO-66 composites were synthesized by the following synthetic route. The preparation method of CDs@UiO-66 is similar to that of UiO-66 (In the supplementary information). The detailed process is that 2 mL of the prepared CDs solution, 1,4-phthalic acid (PTA, 754 mg), ZrCl4 (757 mg) and acetic acid (4 mL) were mixed in DMF (90 mL) and ultrasonically treated for 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After washed with DMF and ethanol for several times, the desired product was dried at 85 °C for 24 h under vacuum.

Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was prepared by referring to the reported method [58]. CDs@UiO-66 (50 mg), Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.223 g) were mixed in ethanol and stirred at 25 °C for 24 h. The resulting Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was washed several times with DMF and ethanol to remove excess Eu3+. Finally, the product was collected by filtration and dried at 60 °C for 24 h.
Syntheses of all materials are illustrated in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ in an aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection of Cu2+ was conducted in deionized water at room temperature. 80 mg Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was dispersed in 80 mL DI-water and sonicated for 10 min to obtain a uniform Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 suspension. Then, Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 suspension was uniformly dispersed into aqueous solutions containing different metal ions (K+, Cd2+, Ag+, Na+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+, 10−2 M), and their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. The different concentrations of Cu2+ were added to Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 suspensions and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 10 min at room temperature. Finally, the anti-interference ability of the sensor was further studied for effective detection of Cu2+ by mixing Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 with other various coexisting metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further confirm the practicability of the probe, we spiked Cu2+ standard solutions into in the tap water from in laboratory and Yellow River water samples (Lanzhou, Gansu Province) and determine Cu2+ with standard addition method. Firstly, the tap water samples and Yellow River water samples were pretreated through a 0.22 µm injection filter. Then a series of different amounts of Cu2+ standard solution was spiked into the sample solutions for fluorescence determination.
Chemicals used in experiment, and instruments and methods for material characterizations were provided in Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of reaction temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of hydrothermal reaction temperature and time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were investigated. CDs were prepared at different temperatures (160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C). When the reaction time was 6 h, the fluorescence emission spectrum was measured. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increased with the increase of reaction temperature in Fig. 1 a. Considering the safety risk caused by the high pressure and high temperature in hydrothermal reaction, the reaction temperature was selected as 200 °C. CDs was also prepared by changing the reaction time (2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h) at 200 °C, and the fluorescence emission spectra of corresponding CDs were recorded. The fluorescence intensity increased with the extension of the reaction time and reached the maximum value at 6 h. Subsequently the fluorescence intensity kept constant as showed in Fig. 1 b. During CDs synthesis, reaction temperature and time influence the size, crystallinity and resulted emission characteristics of CDs. In addition, different reaction temperatures and times lead to distinctive emissive behaviors owing to the different degrees of carbonization. With the increase of reaction temperature and reaction time, the carbonization degree gradually increases, which makes the fluorescence intensity increase. Eventually the optimal preparation conditions of CDs were determined as the reaction temperature of 200 °C and the reaction time of 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CDs@UiO-66 and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs, CDs@UiO-66 and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 were characterized using FT-IR, TEM and SEM. The morphology of the synthesized CDs was observed by TEM and SEM in Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2 b, which illustrated that the CDs had obvious agglomeration phenomenon. As seen from Fig. 1 c and Fig. 1 d the morphology of UiO-66 did not change significantly before and after doping CDs and Eu3+, and the original octahedral structure was retained, indicating that the incorporation of CDs and Eu3+ does not change or affect the original morphology characteristics of the precursor UiO-66 to a great extent. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1 e, the obtained EDS element mapping showed that C, O, N, Zr and Eu elements were uniformly distributed in the particles (The contents of related elements and elemental composition of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 were also detected and exhibited in Fig. S1).

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the XRD pattern of UiO-66 had three characteristic peaks at 7.35°, 8.43° and 25.65°, which represented (111), (200) and (600) crystal plane diffractions respectively. The XRD patterns of the synthesized UiO-66, CDs@UiO-66 and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 were all in good agreement with the simulated XRD pattern, proving that the synthesis of UiO-66 was successful, which also indicated that the skeleton structure of UiO-66 did not change after the addition of CDs and Eu3+. The functional groups on UiO-66, CDs@UiO-66 and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 surface were characterized by the FT-IR spectra in Fig. 3 b. In the FT-IR spectra of CDs, the absorption bands appeared at ∼3416 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of N-H, and characteristic wagging vibrations of N-H can be easily seen at ∼800 cm−1. The peak at 1707 cm−1 belonged to the typical stretching mode of CO. Additionally, the peaks located at ∼1566 and ∼1518 cm−1 resulted from asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of -COO in the MOF, respectively. All the above characteristic peaks existed in Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66, which proved that CDs and UiO-66 coexisted in Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 and further indicated the successful preparation of the composite material. TGA analysis of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was performed under N2 protection (Fig. 3 c). The first weight loss step was attributed to the loss of small amounts of water and ethanol adsorbed on the surface and cavities of the Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 structural material. The second step occurred in the range of 200 ℃∼ 400 ℃, which was assigned to the decomposition of CDs and a partial dissociation of organic ligands in Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66. In the third step of weight loss after 400 ℃, the serious mass loss may result from the collapse of the framework. The above analysis showed that the composite Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 had a good thermal stability. In order to explore the stability of the composite in aqueous solution, it was immersed in aqueous solution for different days and then characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 3 d, the XRD peaks of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 immersed for different days remained basically unchanged. It was speculated that the composite also had an outstanding stability in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential distribution
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size distributions and Zeta potentials of the CDs, CDs@UiO-66, Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 were also measured, and the results are showed in Fig. 4. It can be seen from Fig. 4 a that the particle size distributions were relatively uniform and the average particle sizes were calculated to be about 352.1 ± 4.0 nm, 502.1 ± 3.0 nm, 466.2 ± 6.0 nm, respectively. And the average zeta potentials were −24.8 ± 0.4 mV, −26.9 ± 0.3 mV, −26.6 ± 0.1 mV, respectively (Fig. 4 b). Negative Zeta potential suggested that the composite possessed strong electrostatic attraction to cations. In addition, in our observation, the suspension was stable and less precipitated. These results indicated that Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was suitable for the preparation of nanosuspension and the system had certain stability.

The fluorescent spectra of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 aqueous suspension were evaluated at room temperature. As displayed from Fig. 5 a, at 299 nm excitation wavelength, the Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 show five Eu3+ characteristic emissions at 580, 590, 615, 643, and 697 nm, which are attributed to 5D0→7F0, 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2, 5D0→7F3, and 5D0→7F4 transitions, respectively, besides CDs characteristic fluorescence at about 394 nm. Among these emissions, the red emission peak at 615 nm generated by the 5D0→7F2 transition is supersensitive and typical for Eu3+. In addition, the Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 has obvious adsorption from ultraviolet to visible regions. The characteristic peak at 252 nm is attributed to the π -π* transition of CC. Fig. 5 b showed the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, UiO-66, CDs@UiO-66, and Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 composites at the excitation wavelength of 299 nm. Under the same conditions, the emission peak of single CDs and UiO-66 were weak, but the characteristic emission peak of the UiO-66 modified by CDs was stronger than that of the original. Notably, Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 simultaneously presents two obvious emission peaks at 394 nm and 615 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 c, when the excitation wavelength increases from 280 nm to 320 nm, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 in solution increases slowly and then decreases gradually, eventually reaches its maximum value at 300 nm. This suggests coexistence of different organic functional groups in the composite.

In addition, the luminescence properties of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 under extreme condition were further studied to further evaluate the stability of the material. The fluorescence responses of the Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 probe under different storage times, pH values, and NaCl concentrations were researched and shown in Fig. 6. As showed in Fig. 6 a–b, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F394/F615) hardly changes when the composite is stored at room temperature for 7 days. Fig. 6 c–d demonstrates that pH has almost no effect on the ratiometric fluorescence intensities (F394/F615) when pH increases from 4 to 10, indicating that the composite material has good stability in a wide pH range and can be used in acidic to alkaline environments. In addition, the effect of NaCl concentration was further investigated. As displayed in Fig. 6 e–f, when the concentration of NaCl is in the range of 0.2–0.8 mol/L, F394/F615 basically remains unchanged, indicating that Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 have good salt resistance. Through the above researches, we can conclude that Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 has good stability and can be effectively used in metal ions detection, indicating that the sensor has great potential in practical application.

Firstly, the fluorescence quenching of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 by different cations was studied. Fluorescent spectra of the composite suspensions in the presence of different metal ions including K+, Cd2+, Ag+, Na+, Mn2+, Mg2+,Ca2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+,Cr3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ were showed in Fig. 7 a and the corresponding ratiometric fluorescence intensities (F394/F615) were given in Fig. 7 b. Obviously, the luminescence at 394 nm and 613 nm showed varying degrees of quenching effect after K+, Cd2+, Ag+, Na+, Mn2+, Mg2+,Ca2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ was added respectively, especially Cr3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+ ion exhibited the most drastic quenching effect at 394 nm and 613 nm. However, only Cu2+ showed almost unchanged fluorescence intensity at 394 nm and almost completely quenched fluorescence at 613 nm, so the sensor could be used for selective detection of Cu2+. The fluorescence emission spectra before and after Cu2+ addition were compared in Fig. 7 c. It can be clearly observed that in the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 at 615 nm is almost completely quenched, while the fluorescence of CDs keeps almost unchanged at 394 nm. From the inset, a distinct color change from red to blue is observed under UV-light when Cu2+ is added, which suggests a possibility of visual detection of Cu2+. The specific coordinates in the CIE chroma diagram (Fig. 7 d) moved from the red region (X1 =0.298, Y1 =0.1491) to the blue region (X2 =0.191, Y2 =0.082) after Cu2+ ions were added. Therefore, the above studies indicated that Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 can achieve specific selection for Cu2+.

Subsequently, the high sensitivity of the sensor to Cu2+ detection was evaluated in Fig. 8 a and Fig. 8 b. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 at 615 nm decreased gradually with the increase of Cu2+ concentration from 0 to 500 μM. A good linear relationship between the ratio fluorescence intensity (F394/F615) and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–500 μM (R2 = 0.9969) was obtained in Fig. 8 b and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated as about 51 nM based on the equation LOD = 3Sb/Ksv. The detection limit was much lower than the US Environmental Protection Agency's requirement of 20 μM for the concentration of Cu2+ ions in drinking water. An analytical parameter comparison of the sensor with the previously reported similar results was given in Table 1, and it was found that the developed method here possessed a relatively lower detection limit and wider linear range for Cu2+.

The ratiometric fluorescence sensor Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 can effectively overcome the interference of external environment such as pH and other factors and have high sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ detection. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 have good application prospect in the detection of Cu2+ in water environment. In the presence of other metal ions, Cu2+ ions also showed a similar behavior for the luminescence of the Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 suspension (Fig. S2), which further indicated that Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 could selectively sense Cu2+ in aqueous solution and the sensor has a good anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in the actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 as Cu2+ sensor is further discussed by adding Cu2+ in the tap water and Yellow River water samples. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries of Cu2+ in tap water were 97.35∼100.9 % with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) from 1.64 % to 2.66 % and the recoveries in Yellow River water samples were 98.20∼101.5 % with RSDs of 2.13–2.54 %, indicating the Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 sensor can be successfully applied to the detection of Cu2+ in actual water samples with satisfactory results.

The possible mechanism of Cu2+ induced fluorescence quenching of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 composite was analyzed from the following aspects. The Cu2+ treated composite was characterized by FT-IR and XRD. The FT-IR spectra of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 composite before and after Cu2+ treatment are shown in Fig. S3. Through analysis, it can be seen that the stretching vibration absorption peak of the CO bond at 1580 cm−1 is significantly weakened, which may be caused by the coordination between Cu2+ and oxygen of the ligand. At the same time, the XRD pattern of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 composite treated with Cu2+ aqueous solution is significantly changed compared with that of the original sample, as shown in Fig. S4. The results showed that the crystal structure of the composite is damaged after Cu2+ aqueous solution treatment. In addition, it is easier for Cu2+ to obtain electrons in the unsaturated electron state (3d9). This electron state may promote the interaction between Cu2+ ion and the Lewis basic carboxylic oxygen sites within Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66, and reduce the antenna efficiency between ligand and Eu3+ through binding interaction, so the characteristic emission of Eu3+ at 615 nm is quenched ( Fig. 9). This procedure may lead to the remarkable fluorescence quenching of the Eu3+. However, CDs released from the original structure still retains its fluorescence properties, so the fluorescence emission intensity at 394 nm keeps basically stable. Besides, the SEM image of Fig. S5 showed that the material changed from loose and granular to dense after Cu2+ ion treatment, and the particle feels disappeared. The EDS distribution further explains the existence of Cu2+.

In conclusion, CDs and Eu3+ were respectively introduced into UiO-66 by post-synthetic modification, and a novel ratiometric fluorescent material Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 was prepared. CDs were prepared using green and economical materials and non-toxic solvents for environmental protection purpose and CDs and Eu3+ were employed as emission centers to achieve dual emission. More importantly, the prepared Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 can be used as a highly selective and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ ions in water samples. In the presence of Cu2+ ions, the blue fluorescence of Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66 is almost unchanged at 394 nm, while the red fluorescence of Eu3+ is obviously quenched at 615 nm. As a result, a simple, selective, sensitive and low-cost fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ has been successfully constructed. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor has good stability and can overcome the interference of environmental factors such as ionic strength, pH value, storage time. The proposed method could be exploited to detect Cu2+ in water with a wide linear range (0–500 μM) and a low detection limit (51 nM). Therefore, this method has a very good application prospect in environmental monitoring.

Liping Peng: Writing - review & editing. Hao Guo: Funding acquisition. Ning Wu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yinsheng Liu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Bingqing Liu: Formal analysis, Software. Mingyue Wang: Project administration, Investigation, Resources. Yuan Chen: Project administration, Investigation, Resources. Jiaying Tian：Investigation, Resources. Wu Yang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (20JR10RA083), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23), the Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.131205.

ce:section.ce:para:

.
